source_text: The house is big and the book is new.

reference_translations.text: La casa è grande e il libro è nuovo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. grande is invariable in gender; nuovo agrees masculine with libro.

reference_translations.text: La casa è grande e il libro è nuovo, come vedi.
reference_translations.notes: Padding aside, same core.

reference_translations.text: Il casa è grande e la libro è nuovo.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG genders: casa is feminine (la), libro masculine (il).

explanation: 'The house is big and the book is new' becomes 'La casa è grande e il libro è nuovo.' Casa is feminine (la casa) and libro masculine (il libro); the endings -a and -o set the default gender and the articles follow.

source_text: The flower on the table is red.

reference_translations.text: Il fiore sul tavolo è rosso.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. fiore is masculine, so il fiore and rosso.

reference_translations.text: La fiore sul tavolo è rossa.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: fiore is masculine, not feminine.

explanation: 'The flower on the table is red' becomes 'Il fiore sul tavolo è rosso.' Fiore ends in -e, and its gender must be learned: it is masculine, so il fiore and rosso (not la / rossa).

source_text: La notte è lunga in inverno.

reference_translations.text: The night is long in winter.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Nights are long in winter.
reference_translations.notes: Acceptable generalising rendering.

explanation: 'La notte è lunga in inverno' means 'The night is long in winter.' Notte is a feminine -e noun (la notte), and lunga shows the feminine agreement.

source_text: The problem is serious but the program works.

reference_translations.text: Il problema è serio ma il programma funziona.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Both are masculine Greek -ma nouns.

reference_translations.text: La problema è seria ma la programma funziona.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: problema and programma are masculine despite -a.

explanation: 'The problem is serious but the program works' becomes 'Il problema è serio ma il programma funziona.' Problema and programma end in -a but are masculine (the Greek -ma group), so il problema, il programma, and serio agrees masculine.

source_text: I hurt my hand at work.

reference_translations.text: Mi sono fatto male alla mano al lavoro.
reference_translations.notes: Male speaker. mano is feminine, so a + la = alla mano.

reference_translations.text: Mi sono fatta male alla mano al lavoro.
reference_translations.notes: Female speaker; participle fatta.

reference_translations.text: Mi sono fatto male al mano al lavoro.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: mano is feminine, so alla, not al.

explanation: 'I hurt my hand at work' becomes 'Mi sono fatto/a male alla mano al lavoro.' Mano ends in -o but is feminine, so the articulated preposition is alla (a + la), not al; the feminine surfaces on the little words around the noun.

source_text: Ti mando la foto domani.

reference_translations.text: I'll send you the photo tomorrow.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: I'm sending you the photo tomorrow.
reference_translations.notes: Present-for-future also fine.

explanation: 'Ti mando la foto domani' means 'I'll send you the photo tomorrow.' Foto is feminine (short for fotografia), hence la foto even though it ends in -o.

source_text: The books are on the tables.

reference_translations.text: I libri sono sui tavoli.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. -o to -i on both nouns; su + i = sui.

reference_translations.text: I libros sono sui tavolos.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: English -s; Italian is libri, tavoli.

explanation: 'The books are on the tables' becomes 'I libri sono sui tavoli.' Both libro and tavolo are masculine -o nouns, so the plural is -i: libri, tavoli.

source_text: My sisters have three cats.

reference_translations.text: Le mie sorelle hanno tre gatti.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. sorella to sorelle (-a/-e), gatto to gatti (-o/-i).

reference_translations.text: Le mie sorelli hanno tre gatte.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: sorella is feminine, plural sorelle, not sorelli.

explanation: 'My sisters have three cats' becomes 'Le mie sorelle hanno tre gatti.' Feminine sorella takes -e (sorelle); masculine gatto takes -i (gatti): the two regular endings side by side.

source_text: Le case nuove sono care.

reference_translations.text: The new houses are expensive.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: New houses are expensive.
reference_translations.notes: Article-less generic reading, acceptable.

explanation: 'Le case nuove sono care' means 'The new houses are expensive.' Casa to case is the -a to -e plural, and nuove and care agree feminine plural.

source_text: I bought two kilos of oranges.

reference_translations.text: Ho comprato due chili di arance.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. chilo to chili; arancia to arance (i dropped).

reference_translations.text: Ho comprato due chili di arancie.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: consonant before -cia, so the i is dropped: arance.

explanation: 'I bought two kilos of oranges' becomes 'Ho comprato due chili di arance.' Arancia has a consonant before the -cia, so the plural drops the i: arance, not arancie.

source_text: My friends are Greek.

reference_translations.text: I miei amici sono greci.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Both keep the soft sound: amici, greci.

reference_translations.text: I miei amichi sono grechi.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: no h here; amico to amici, greco to greci.

explanation: 'My friends are Greek' becomes 'I miei amici sono greci.' Amico and greco both take the soft -ci plural (no h): amici, greci.

source_text: Le spiagge italiane sono belle.

reference_translations.text: Italian beaches are beautiful.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: The Italian beaches are beautiful.
reference_translations.notes: With article, also fine.

explanation: 'Le spiagge italiane sono belle' means 'Italian beaches are beautiful.' Spiaggia has a consonant before -gia, so the plural drops the i: spiagge.

source_text: The lakes in the mountains are cold.

reference_translations.text: I laghi in montagna sono freddi.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. lago to laghi keeps the hard g with h.

reference_translations.text: I lagi in montagna sono freddi.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: without h the g goes soft; the plural is laghi.

explanation: 'The lakes in the mountains are cold' becomes 'I laghi in montagna sono freddi.' Lago adds an h to keep the hard g: laghi.

source_text: Italian cities are beautiful.

reference_translations.text: Le città italiane sono belle.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. città is invariable; the article and adjective carry the plural.

reference_translations.text: Le cittàs italiane sono belle.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: accented finals never add -s; città stays città.

explanation: 'Italian cities are beautiful' becomes 'Le città italiane sono belle.' Città has a stressed accented final and does not change; only le and belle show the plural.

source_text: Ho visto due bei film ieri sera.

reference_translations.text: I saw two good films last night.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. film is invariable in Italian.

reference_translations.text: I watched two good films last night.
reference_translations.notes: watch/see both fine.

explanation: 'Ho visto due bei film ieri sera' means 'I saw two good films last night.' Film is a borrowed word and stays the same in the plural: due film, not due filmi.

source_text: We ate eggs for breakfast.

reference_translations.text: Abbiamo mangiato le uova a colazione.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. uovo to uova (masculine to feminine plural).

reference_translations.text: Abbiamo mangiato gli uovi a colazione.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the plural is le uova, not gli uovi.

explanation: 'We ate eggs for breakfast' becomes 'Abbiamo mangiato le uova a colazione.' Uovo is masculine but its plural is the feminine uova, so le uova.

source_text: She has strong hands.

reference_translations.text: Ha le mani forti.
reference_translations.notes: Standard, subject dropped. mano to mani, feminine.

reference_translations.text: Lei ha le mani forti.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun kept.

reference_translations.text: Ha i mani forti.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: mani is feminine, so le mani, not i mani.

explanation: 'She has strong hands' becomes 'Ha le mani forti.' Mano is feminine and its plural is the irregular mani; the article le confirms the feminine.

source_text: Mi fanno male le braccia.

reference_translations.text: My arms hurt.
reference_translations.notes: Standard, body sense.

reference_translations.text: My arms are hurting.
reference_translations.notes: Progressive, also fine.

explanation: 'Mi fanno male le braccia' means 'My arms hurt.' For the body, braccio takes the feminine plural braccia (le braccia), distinct from the mechanical bracci.

source_text: There are many men in the square.

reference_translations.text: Ci sono molti uomini in piazza.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. uomo to uomini (suppletive).

reference_translations.text: Ci sono molti uomi in piazza.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the plural is uomini, not uomi.

explanation: 'There are many men in the square' becomes 'Ci sono molti uomini in piazza.' Uomo has the suppletive plural uomini, so molti uomini.

source_text: I need two keyrings.

reference_translations.text: Mi servono due portachiavi.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. portachiavi is invariable (the noun part chiavi is already plural).

reference_translations.text: Mi servono due portachiavii.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: portachiavi cannot pluralise; it is invariable.

explanation: 'I need two keyrings' becomes 'Mi servono due portachiavi.' Portachiavi (porta + chiavi) already contains a plural noun, so the compound is invariable: due portachiavi.

source_text: Ho due amici a Roma.

reference_translations.text: I have two friends in Rome.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

explanation: 'Ho due amici a Roma' means 'I have two friends in Rome.' Amico takes the soft -ci plural, amici (no h).

source_text: Il pane è fresco e la chiave è sul tavolo.

reference_translations.text: The bread is fresh and the key is on the table.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

explanation: 'Il pane è fresco e la chiave è sul tavolo' means 'The bread is fresh and the key is on the table.' Two -e nouns of opposite gender: il pane (m) and la chiave (f), each shown by its article.
